2.play: ANTONIO: In sooth, I know not why I am so sad:

3.play: It wearies me; you say it wearies you;

4.play: But how I caught it, found it, or came by it,

5.play: What stuff 'tis made of, whereof it is born,

6.play: I am to learn;

7.play: And such a want-wit sadness makes of me,

8.play: That I have much ado to know myself.

9.play: SALARINO: Your mind is tossing on the ocean;

10.play: There, where your argosies with portly sail,

11.play: Like signiors and rich burghers on the flood,

12.play: Or, as it were, the pageants of the sea,

13.play: Do overpeer the petty traffickers,

14.play: That curtsy to them, do them reverence,

15.play: As they fly by them with their woven wings.

16.play: SALANIO: Believe me, sir, had I such venture forth,

17.play: The better part of my affections would

18.play: Be with my hopes abroad. I should be still

19.play: Plucking the grass, to know where sits the wind,

20.play: Peering in maps for ports and piers and roads;

21.play: And every object that might make me fear

22.play: Misfortune to my ventures, out of doubt

24.play: SALARINO: My wind cooling my broth

25.play: Would blow me to an ague, when I thought

26.play: What harm a wind too great at sea might do.

27.play: I should not see the sandy hour-glass run,

28.play: But I should think of shallows and of flats,

29.play: And see my wealthy Andrew dock'd in sand,

30.play: Vailing her high-top lower than her ribs

31.play: To kiss her burial. Should I go to church

32.play: And see the holy edifice of stone,

33.play: And not bethink me straight of dangerous rocks,

34.play: Which touching but my gentle vessel's side,

35.play: Would scatter all her spices on the stream,

36.play: Enrobe the roaring waters with my silks,

37.play: And, in a word, but even now worth this,

38.play: And now worth nothing? Shall I have the thought

39.play: To think on this, and shall I lack the thought

40.play: That such a thing bechanced would make me sad?

41.play: But tell not me; I know, Antonio

42.play: Is sad to think upon his merchandise.

43.play: ANTONIO: Believe me, no: I thank my fortune for it,

44.play: My ventures are not in one bottom trusted,

45.play: Nor to one place; nor is my whole estate

46.play: Upon the fortune of this present year:

47.play: Therefore my merchandise makes me not sad.

48.play: SALARINO: Why, then you are in love.

50.play: SALARINO: Not in love neither? Then let us say you are sad,

51.play: Because you are not merry: and 'twere as easy

52.play: For you to laugh and leap and say you are merry,

53.play: Because you are not sad. Now, by two-headed Janus,

54.play: Nature hath framed strange fellows in her time:

55.play: Some that will evermore peep through their eyes

56.play: And laugh like parrots at a bag-piper,

57.play: And other of such vinegar aspect

58.play: That they'll not show their teeth in way of smile,

59.play: Though Nestor swear the jest be laughable.

60.play: SALANIO: Here comes Bassanio, your most noble kinsman,

61.play: Gratiano and Lorenzo. Fare ye well:

62.play: We leave you now with better company.

63.play: SALARINO: I would have stay'd till I had made you merry,

64.play: If worthier friends had not prevented me.

65.play: ANTONIO: Your worth is very dear in my regard.

66.play: I take it, your own business calls on you

67.play: And you embrace the occasion to depart.

68.play: SALARINO: Good morrow, my good lords.

69.play: BASSANIO: Good signiors both, when shall we laugh? say, when?

70.play: You grow exceeding strange: must it be so?

71.play: SALARINO: We'll make our leisures to attend on yours.

72.play: LORENZO: My Lord Bassanio, since you have found Antonio,

73.play: We two will leave you: but at dinner-time,

74.play: I pray you, have in mind where we must meet.

75.play: BASSANIO: I will not fail you.

76.play: GRATIANO: You look not well, Signior Antonio;

77.play: You have too much respect upon the world:

78.play: They lose it that do buy it with much care:

79.play: Believe me, you are marvellously changed.

80.play: ANTONIO: I hold the world but as the world, Gratiano;

81.play: A stage where every man must play a part,

83.play: GRATIANO: Let me play the fool:

84.play: With mirth and laughter let old wrinkles come,

85.play: And let my liver rather heat with wine

86.play: Than my heart cool with mortifying groans.

87.play: Why should a man, whose blood is warm within,

88.play: Sit like his grandsire cut in alabaster?

89.play: Sleep when he wakes and creep into the jaundice

90.play: By being peevish? I tell thee what, Antonio--

91.play: I love thee, and it is my love that speaks--

92.play: There are a sort of men whose visages

93.play: Do cream and mantle like a standing pond,

94.play: And do a wilful stillness entertain,

95.play: With purpose to be dress'd in an opinion

97.play: As who should say 'I am Sir Oracle,

98.play: And when I ope my lips let no dog bark!'

99.play: O my Antonio, I do know of these

100.play: That therefore only are reputed wise

101.play: For saying nothing; when, I am very sure,

102.play: If they should speak, would almost damn those ears,

103.play: Which, hearing them, would call their brothers fools.

104.play: I'll tell thee more of this another time:

105.play: But fish not, with this melancholy bait,

106.play: For this fool gudgeon, this opinion.

107.play: Come, good Lorenzo. Fare ye well awhile:

108.play: I'll end my exhortation after dinner.

109.play: LORENZO: Well, we will leave you then till dinner-time:

110.play: I must be one of these same dumb wise men,

111.play: For Gratiano never lets me speak.

112.play: GRATIANO: Well, keep me company but two years moe,

113.play: Thou shalt not know the sound of thine own tongue.

114.play: ANTONIO: Farewell: I'll grow a talker for this gear.

115.play: GRATIANO: Thanks, i' faith, for silence is only commendable

116.play: In a neat's tongue dried and a maid not vendible.

117.play: ANTONIO: Is that any thing now?

118.play: BASSANIO: Gratiano speaks an infinite deal of nothing, more

119.play: than any man in all Venice. His reasons are as two

120.play: grains of wheat hid in two bushels of chaff: you

121.play: shall seek all day ere you find them, and when you

122.play: have them, they are not worth the search.

123.play: ANTONIO: Well, tell me now what lady is the same

124.play: To whom you swore a secret pilgrimage,

125.play: That you to-day promised to tell me of?

126.play: BASSANIO: 'Tis not unknown to you, Antonio,

127.play: How much I have disabled mine estate,

128.play: By something showing a more swelling port

129.play: Than my faint means would grant continuance:

130.play: Nor do I now make moan to be abridged

131.play: From such a noble rate; but my chief care

132.play: Is to come fairly off from the great debts

133.play: Wherein my time something too prodigal

134.play: Hath left me gaged. To you, Antonio,

135.play: I owe the most, in money and in love,

136.play: And from your love I have a warranty

137.play: To unburden all my plots and purposes

138.play: How to get clear of all the debts I owe.

139.play: ANTONIO: I pray you, good Bassanio, let me know it;

140.play: And if it stand, as you yourself still do,

141.play: Within the eye of honour, be assured,

142.play: My purse, my person, my extremest means,

143.play: Lie all unlock'd to your occasions.

144.play: BASSANIO: In my school-days, when I had lost one shaft,

145.play: I shot his fellow of the self-same flight

146.play: The self-same way with more advised watch,

147.play: To find the other forth, and by adventuring both

148.play: I oft found both: I urge this childhood proof,

149.play: Because what follows is pure innocence.

150.play: I owe you much, and, like a wilful youth,

151.play: That which I owe is lost; but if you please

152.play: To shoot another arrow that self way

153.play: Which you did shoot the first, I do not doubt,

154.play: As I will watch the aim, or to find both

155.play: Or bring your latter hazard back again

156.play: And thankfully rest debtor for the first.

157.play: ANTONIO: You know me well, and herein spend but time

158.play: To wind about my love with circumstance;

159.play: And out of doubt you do me now more wrong

160.play: In making question of my uttermost

161.play: Than if you had made waste of all I have:

162.play: Then do but say to me what I should do

163.play: That in your knowledge may by me be done,

164.play: And I am prest unto it: therefore, speak.

165.play: BASSANIO: In Belmont is a lady richly left;

166.play: And she is fair, and, fairer than that word,

167.play: Of wondrous virtues: sometimes from her eyes

168.play: I did receive fair speechless messages:

169.play: Her name is Portia, nothing undervalued

171.play: Nor is the wide world ignorant of her worth,

172.play: For the four winds blow in from every coast

173.play: Renowned suitors, and her sunny locks

174.play: Hang on her temples like a golden fleece;

175.play: Which makes her seat of Belmont Colchos' strand,

176.play: And many Jasons come in quest of her.

177.play: O my Antonio, had I but the means

178.play: To hold a rival place with one of them,

179.play: I have a mind presages me such thrift,

180.play: That I should questionless be fortunate!

181.play: ANTONIO: Thou know'st that all my fortunes are at sea;

182.play: Neither have I money nor commodity

183.play: To raise a present sum: therefore go forth;

184.play: Try what my credit can in Venice do:

185.play: That shall be rack'd, even to the uttermost,

186.play: To furnish thee to Belmont, to fair Portia.

187.play: Go, presently inquire, and so will I,

188.play: Where money is, and I no question make

189.play: To have it of my trust or for my sake.

173.play: GRATIANO: Now, by this hand, I gave it to a youth,

174.play: A kind of boy, a little scrubbed boy,

175.play: No higher than thyself; the judge's clerk,

176.play: A prating boy, that begg'd it as a fee:

177.play: I could not for my heart deny it him.

178.play: PORTIA: You were to blame, I must be plain with you,

179.play: To part so slightly with your wife's first gift:

180.play: A thing stuck on with oaths upon your finger

181.play: And so riveted with faith unto your flesh.

182.play: I gave my love a ring and made him swear

183.play: Never to part with it; and here he stands;

184.play: I dare be sworn for him he would not leave it

185.play: Nor pluck it from his finger, for the wealth

186.play: That the world masters. Now, in faith, Gratiano,

187.play: You give your wife too unkind a cause of grief:

188.play: An 'twere to me, I should be mad at it.

189.play: BASSANIO: [Aside]  Why, I were best to cut my left hand off

190.play: And swear I lost the ring defending it.

191.play: GRATIANO: My Lord Bassanio gave his ring away

192.play: Unto the judge that begg'd it and indeed

193.play: Deserved it too; and then the boy, his clerk,

194.play: That took some pains in writing, he begg'd mine;

195.play: And neither man nor master would take aught

197.play: PORTIA: What ring gave you my lord?

198.play: Not that, I hope, which you received of me.

199.play: BASSANIO: If I could add a lie unto a fault,

200.play: I would deny it; but you see my finger

211.play: And how unwillingly I left the ring,

212.play: When nought would be accepted but the ring,

213.play: You would abate the strength of your displeasure.

214.play: PORTIA: If you had known the virtue of the ring,

215.play: Or half her worthiness that gave the ring,

216.play: Or your own honour to contain the ring,

217.play: You would not then have parted with the ring.

218.play: What man is there so much unreasonable,

219.play: If you had pleased to have defended it

220.play: With any terms of zeal, wanted the modesty

222.play: Nerissa teaches me what to believe:

223.play: I'll die for't but some woman had the ring.

224.play: BASSANIO: No, by my honour, madam, by my soul,

225.play: No woman had it, but a civil doctor,

226.play: Which did refuse three thousand ducats of me

227.play: And begg'd the ring; the which I did deny him

228.play: And suffer'd him to go displeased away;

229.play: Even he that did uphold the very life

230.play: Of my dear friend. What should I say, sweet lady?

231.play: I was enforced to send it after him;

232.play: I was beset with shame and courtesy;

233.play: My honour would not let ingratitude

234.play: So much besmear it. Pardon me, good lady;

235.play: For, by these blessed candles of the night,

236.play: Had you been there, I think you would have begg'd

237.play: The ring of me to give the worthy doctor.

238.play: PORTIA: Let not that doctor e'er come near my house:

239.play: Since he hath got the jewel that I loved,

240.play: And that which you did swear to keep for me,

241.play: I will become as liberal as you;

242.play: I'll not deny him any thing I have,

243.play: No, not my body nor my husband's bed:

244.play: Know him I shall, I am well sure of it:

245.play: Lie not a night from home; watch me like Argus:

246.play: If you do not, if I be left alone,

247.play: Now, by mine honour, which is yet mine own,

248.play: I'll have that doctor for my bedfellow.

249.play: NERISSA: And I his clerk; therefore be well advised

250.play: How you do leave me to mine own protection.

251.play: GRATIANO: Well, do you so; let not me take him, then;

252.play: For if I do, I'll mar the young clerk's pen.

253.play: ANTONIO: I am the unhappy subject of these quarrels.

254.play: PORTIA: Sir, grieve not you; you are welcome notwithstanding.

255.play: BASSANIO: Portia, forgive me this enforced wrong;

256.play: And, in the hearing of these many friends,

257.play: I swear to thee, even by thine own fair eyes,

260.play: In both my eyes he doubly sees himself;

261.play: In each eye, one: swear by your double self,

262.play: And there's an oath of credit.

264.play: Pardon this fault, and by my soul I swear

265.play: I never more will break an oath with thee.

266.play: ANTONIO: I once did lend my body for his wealth;

267.play: Which, but for him that had your husband's ring,

268.play: Had quite miscarried: I dare be bound again,

269.play: My soul upon the forfeit, that your lord

270.play: Will never more break faith advisedly.

274.play: BASSANIO: By heaven, it is the same I gave the doctor!

275.play: PORTIA: I had it of him: pardon me, Bassanio;

276.play: For, by this ring, the doctor lay with me.

277.play: NERISSA: And pardon me, my gentle Gratiano;

278.play: For that same scrubbed boy, the doctor's clerk,

281.play: In summer, where the ways are fair enough:

282.play: What, are we cuckolds ere we have deserved it?

283.play: PORTIA: Speak not so grossly. You are all amazed:

291.play: And I have better news in store for you

292.play: Than you expect: unseal this letter soon;

295.play: You shall not know by what strange accident

316.play: And yet I am sure you are not satisfied

317.play: Of these events at full. Let us go in;

321.play: That my Nerissa shall be sworn on is,

322.play: Whether till the next night she had rather stay,

323.play: Or go to bed now, being two hours to day:

324.play: But were the day come, I should wish it dark,
